text: Welcome to This is my Architecture. Today I'm pleased to have Bastian Widmer from Amazie.io.
Hi Bastian. Hey, thank you for having me. Thank you for being here. Okay, Bastian,
can you tell us a bit about Amazie.io and Lagoon? Certainly. At Amazie.io, we built Lagoon,
which is our open source application delivery platform, where we help developers build their
applications and move it into Kubernetes without actually needing too much knowledge on how
Kubernetes works inside. Okay, great. So maybe you can explain us the flow and all your developers
can get an environment on your platform? Yeah. So here we have the developers. They basically
create code and push that into their Git repositories. So in the Git repository, we have Webhooks connected to Lagoon.
Lagoon listens on those repositories and decides what needs to be done. For example, as we're using
infrastructure as a code, as a paradigm, like everything you want to see deployed is defined in your Git repository.
So Lagoon sees, oh, you want a feature branch or it's easy. Oh, you opened a pull request. So it will go
to the hosting zones that we will talk into a bit and then deploy everything we need.
Okay. So I can see that you leverage EKS both for Lagoon, the core of your application, and for the
environment that it will provide to your developers. Can you tell us a bit more about EKS and why you choose
this service? Yes. So we started a few years ago with our journey into Kubernetes. Back then, Kubernetes
didn't have all the features we needed. So we started out with the whole infrastructure in OpenShift.
As over time, more and more features that we had in OpenShift were re-implemented in Kubernetes,
we started to move into Kubernetes. So that's basically where we ended up. And it gives us much more
flexibility and the possibilities to stay on the bleeding edge of Kubernetes. Great. So here I can
see EC2 and EC2 Spot. I can imagine that you leverage those instances from EKS, right? Yes. So we have two
things. We have our base infrastructure is basically run with normal EC2 instances. So every, like the whole
base workload runs there, we started our journey into Spot instances, for example, for development or
non-productive infrastructure where we can tolerate a failure or termination of one of the pods.
Great. And what was the benefits of EC2 Spot? What did you see?
EC2 versus EC2 Spot, we see a cost benefit between 70 and 90%.
Great. Great. Okay, great. And at the bottom, I can see several storage services. Can you tell us a bit
about those services? Yeah. So as I mentioned, Lagoon tries to understand what the developer wants to do.
What we do there is we try to be as transparent as possible that people don't need to think which
storage they need. So on the EKS cluster, we have an operator running that will figure out,
oh, you need storage. So it can be that you just start storing your files in EFS because it's just normal
assets that don't need anything specific. Or you have a workload that goes into an EBS volume because
it's more specialized. For example, a database or a search server like OpenSearch or Solr.
Okay. Talking about database, I can see Aurora here.
Yes. We also use Aurora. And it can be that the developer says, hey, I need a database. So when
you deploy, during the deployment, Lagoon will, through EKS, tell you, hey, I need a database. And an
operator picks it up and provisions a database for you and exposes it back to the project as a set of
secrets. Okay. Okay, great. So on this database, I can see that you leveraged the Graviton2 instances,
so the R6J. Can you tell us a bit about this?
Yeah. So we were really stoked seeing ARM infrastructure making its way into cloud. And
the logical first step for us was like starting where we see the least harm or like the least
problems. And we started basically using it on non-production environments to see like,
are there any regressions? And we are fairly certain by now that there aren't. So we start to move
like more and more production workload onto our 6Gs. Great, great. And another interesting point here,
you can, you use GP3. So it was announced last year in 2020. When did you try and adopt this technology?
Yeah. So we basically jumped onto GP3 as soon as it got announced because GP3 gives us lower storage
costs and it also helps us like with a better IOPS performance across the board. So we started that
mid-December with a few test workloads and then we used maintenance windows to just roll it out across
every platform. Great, great. Very interesting. Maybe what are your next challenges for this platform?
Our next challenges are basically tying everything back together. So basically moving more from EC2 to
EC2 spot instances. That's something that we're already working on. Using more of the new instances.
It's not everything on R6Gs yet, but we are working on getting there. And then
making it like a well-rounded product and lowering the costs. But the new savings plans help us a lot
doing that. Okay. Thank you. Thank you for sharing, Bastien. You're welcome. Thank you.
And thank you for watching. This is my architecture.
